name: Mario Bauzá Cárdenas
description: Cuban-American musician, composer, and founder of Afro-Cuban Jazz
abstract: Prudencio Mario Bauzá Cárdenas (April 28, 1911 – July 11, 1993) was an Afro-Cuban jazz bandleader, and jazz musician. He was among the first to introduce Cuban music to the United States by bringing Cuban musical styles to the New York City jazz scene.

concerts.label: Concert of Cuban and Puerto Rican Music
Caribbean Expressions Festival '84: Cuba y Puerto Rico Son...
Latin Jazz USA Concert and Awards Presentation

quotes.text: No, no, he played Carnegie Hall. I played intermission.
quotes.caption: Bauza recalls playing intermission while someone else performed

quotes.text: Yeah. They couldn't carry it in Carnegie Hall.
quotes.caption: Brief comment on carrying something into Carnegie Hall

about_quotes.speaker: Wilson "Chembo" Corniel
about_quotes.evidence: Mario Bauza introduced him to Dizzy Gillespie. This is where “Manteca,” “Tin Tin Deo” all those numbers — so if you don’t think Dizzy Gillespie is legitimate enough — he’s the one who is the father of Latin jazz but before him it was Machito and Mario Bauza.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wilson "Chembo" Corniel interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, November 15, 2019

about_quotes.speaker: Ignacio Berroa
about_quotes.evidence: Mario became my mentor, it was Mario the guy that took me to the union musicians in New York. He recommended me with different musicians in New York...
about_quotes.doc_title: Ignacio Berroa interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, October 22, 2021 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Palmieri
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee discusses Mario Bauzá's role in introducing Chano Pozo to Dizzy Gillespie and his work with Machito, but does not describe a direct personal or professional relationship with Bauzá himself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Eddie Palmieri — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Paquito D'Rivera
about_quotes.evidence: Mario Bauza was a dear friend of my father’s... Mario was like kind of waiting here for me; he was a friend of my father from the 30s and 40s... I contact them
about_quotes.doc_title: Paquito D'Rivera — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Teddy McRae
about_quotes.evidence: Let's talk about the people who were in the band when you arrived... The trumpet section, now, included at the time, there was Mario Bauza

about_quotes.speaker: A.B. Spellman
about_quotes.evidence: I mean you follow some very good musicians who Mario Bauza had originally hired to be in the — oh man my brain is leaking
about_quotes.doc_title: A.B. Spellman interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, January 19, 2022
